questions.text: Which Constitutional Amendment added the words 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976), passed during the Emergency under Indira Gandhi, added 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble. The original Preamble (1949) read: 'Sovereign Democratic Republic'. After the 42nd Amendment it reads: 'Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic' and 'unity and integrity of the Nation' (replacing 'unity of the Nation'). This is one of the most consistently tested amendment facts in SSC exams.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the 42nd Amendment and Preamble changes is one of the most frequently tested constitutional amendment questions

questions.text: The 61st Constitutional Amendment Act (1988) is related to:
options: Lowering the voting age from 21 to 18 years
questions.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment Act (1988), which came into force in 1989, lowered the minimum voting age from 21 to 18 years by amending Article 326. This made approximately 3.5 crore young citizens newly eligible to vote in the 1989 general elections. The Anti-Defection Law was the 52nd Amendment (1985); Abolition of privy purses was the 26th Amendment (1971); Fundamental Duties were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976).
questions.source: SSC CGL — the 61st Amendment reducing voting age to 18 is a standard amendment identification question

questions.text: The Right to Property was removed from the list of Fundamental Rights and made a legal right by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 44th Constitutional Amendment Act (1978), passed by the Janata government after the Emergency, removed the Right to Property from Part III (Fundamental Rights) and made it a legal right under Article 300A: 'No person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law.' This was one of the most significant changes — right to property had previously been a FR under Articles 19(1)(f) and 31, frequently used to challenge land reform laws. As a legal right, it is still protected but cannot be enforced through Article 32 (SC writ jurisdiction for FRs).
questions.source: SSC CGL — the 44th Amendment removing Right to Property from FRs is one of the most consistently tested amendment facts

questions.text: The Fundamental Duties were added to the Constitution by the:
options: 42nd Amendment Act, 1976 (10 duties); 86th Amendment Act, 2002 (11th duty)
44th Amendment Act, 1978 (10 duties); 91st Amendment Act, 2003 (11th duty)
52nd Amendment Act, 1985 (10 duties); 86th Amendment Act, 2002 (11th duty)
questions.explanation: The original 10 Fundamental Duties were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976) — as Article 51A in the new Part IVA, based on the Swaran Singh Committee recommendation and inspired by the Soviet Constitution. The 11th Fundamental Duty — 'to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of six and fourteen years' — was added by the 86th Amendment (2002), along with Article 21A (Right to Education). Currently there are 11 Fundamental Duties under Article 51A.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the two-amendment history of Fundamental Duties (42nd + 86th) and the count (10 + 1 = 11) is a standard amendment question

questions.text: The 99th Constitutional Amendment (2014) established the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC). What happened to it?
options: It was implemented successfully and replaced the Collegium system
It was struck down by the Supreme Court in 2015 as it violated judicial independence, which is part of the Basic Structure
It was withdrawn by Parliament before it could come into force
It was referred by the President to the Supreme Court for an advisory opinion under Article 143
questions.explanation: The 99th Amendment created the NJAC — a 6-member body (CJI + 2 senior SC judges + Law Minister + 2 eminent persons) to replace the Collegium system for judicial appointments. The Supreme Court in Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v Union of India (2015) struck it down by a 4:1 majority, holding that the inclusion of the Law Minister and the veto power of the two eminent persons would compromise judicial independence — which is part of the Basic Structure. The Collegium system continues to operate.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the NJAC story (created 2014, struck down 2015) is a significant recent constitutional event that appears in higher-difficulty SSC papers

questions.text: The Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) is important in constitutional law because it:
options: Held that Parliament cannot amend any Fundamental Right
Upheld Parliament's power to amend the Constitution but said it cannot destroy the 'Basic Structure'
Held that the Preamble is justiciable and part of Fundamental Rights
Established that the Supreme Court can strike down any constitutional amendment
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala (1973), a 13-judge Constitution bench ruled 7:6 that Parliament has the power to amend any provision of the Constitution — overruling Golak Nath (1967) which had said FRs cannot be amended. But critically, the court held Parliament cannot destroy the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution. Basic Structure includes: supremacy of the Constitution, republican form, secular character, separation of powers, federalism, judicial review, and free/fair elections. This principle — upholding the 24th Amendment but limiting Parliament's amending power — is the foundational principle of Indian constitutional law.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Kesavananda Bharati's specific holding (Parliament CAN amend but CANNOT destroy Basic Structure) is a nuanced understanding question that appears in higher-difficulty competitive exams

questions.text: For which type of constitutional amendments does Parliament require ratification by at least half of the state legislatures?
options: Amendments affecting federal provisions — such as election of President, distribution of legislative powers, representation in Parliament, SC and HC
questions.explanation: Under Article 368, there are three types of constitutional amendments. Most amendments require only special majority (majority of total membership + 2/3rd present and voting in each House). But amendments affecting 'federal' provisions require, in addition, ratification by legislatures of at least half of the states. Federal provisions include: election of the President (Articles 54-55), executive power of Union/States (Articles 73 and 162), extent of legislative power (Union and State List — Article 246), SC and HCs (Articles 124-147, 214-231), distribution of taxes between Centre and States (Article 268-279A), and representation of states in Parliament. Amendments to FRs do NOT require state ratification — only special majority.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because students often assume all amendments or FR amendments require state ratification; the correct trigger (federal provisions) is a nuanced distinction

questions.text: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) moved which subjects from the State List to the Concurrent List?
options: Education, Forests, and Protection of Wild Animals and Birds
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976) transferred three subjects from the State List to the Concurrent List: (1) Education, (2) Forests, and (3) Protection of Wild Animals and Birds. This is why education is now a Concurrent List subject — it was originally a State List subject. This transfer allowed Parliament to enact laws like the Right to Education Act (2009) and the Wildlife Protection Act (1972, subsequently empowered at the national level) on subjects that had previously been exclusively for state legislatures.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students know 42nd Amendment for Preamble and Fundamental Duties but rarely remember the Seventh Schedule changes

questions.text: The 86th Constitutional Amendment (2002) made which fundamental change to the Right to Life provision?
options: It expanded Article 21 to include the right to privacy
It added a new Article 21A making free and compulsory education (for ages 6-14) a Fundamental Right
It removed reasonable restrictions from Article 21
It added the right to livelihood as part of Article 21
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment (2002) inserted Article 21A: 'The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine.' This made education a Fundamental Right for the first time. It was implemented through the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (RTE) Act, 2009. The same amendment added the 11th Fundamental Duty (Article 51A(k)) requiring parents to provide education opportunities to children aged 6-14, and changed Article 45 (DPSP) to focus on early childhood care for children below 6.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students associate RTE with 2009 (the Act) and miss that the constitutional basis (Article 21A) came from the 86th Amendment of 2002

questions.text: Which of the following was NOT done by the 44th Amendment (1978)?
options: Replaced 'internal disturbance' with 'armed rebellion' as grounds for National Emergency
Made Articles 20 and 21 non-suspendable even during Emergency
Removed the Right to Property from Part III (Fundamental Rights)
questions.explanation: Adding 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble was done by the 42nd Amendment (1976) — not the 44th. The 44th Amendment (1978) was the Janata government's reversal of Emergency-era changes: it (a) changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion' (higher Emergency threshold), (b) made Articles 20 and 21 non-suspendable during Emergency (Article 359 cannot suspend them), and (c) removed the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights and made it Article 300A. The Preamble words are a very common exam trap — students confuse the 42nd and 44th Amendments.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the 42nd vs 44th Amendment confusion is one of the most common mistakes; a 'what was NOT done by 44th' question tests this directly

questions.text: Which of the following is the CORRECT description of the 'special majority' required for most Constitutional Amendments under Article 368?
options: Two-thirds of members present and voting in each House
Majority of total membership AND two-thirds of members present and voting in each House
Three-fourths of total membership in each House
Simple majority in both Houses plus President's special assent
questions.explanation: Under Article 368, a 'special majority' for constitutional amendments means: (a) a majority of the total membership of each House (i.e., more than half the full strength, regardless of vacancies or absentees), AND (b) not less than two-thirds of the members of each House present and voting. BOTH conditions must be met simultaneously. This is stricter than a simple 2/3rd majority because the total membership condition means even absent members count against the bill.
questions.source: The 'what is special majority under Art 368' question is the most frequently tested amendment procedure question in SSC CGL

questions.text: Which amendment act is known as the 'Mini-Constitution' due to the large number of changes it made?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 — passed during the Emergency — is called the 'Mini-Constitution' or 'Constitution of Indira' due to the sweeping changes it made. Key changes: added 'Socialist', 'Secular', 'Integrity' to Preamble; added Fundamental Duties (Part IVA, Article 51A); moved Education, Forests, Wildlife from State to Concurrent List; curtailed judicial review; extended LS/Assembly terms to 6 years; made DPSPs superior to FRs. It was the most ambitious single amendment in Indian constitutional history — and many of its provisions were later reversed or struck down.
questions.source: The 42nd Amendment as 'Mini-Constitution' is a standard identification question that appears in almost every SSC paper touching amendments

questions.text: Article 21A, which makes education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6-14, was inserted by which amendment?
questions.explanation: Article 21A was inserted by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002, making free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14 years. The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (RTE) Act was subsequently enacted in 2009 to implement Article 21A. The same 86th Amendment added the 11th Fundamental Duty (Article 51A(k)) and changed the DPSP Article 45 from 'education of children up to 14' to 'early childhood care for children below 6'. Note: the amendment is 2002, the implementing Act is 2009 — both dates are tested.
questions.source: Article 21A and the 86th Amendment are among the most frequently tested social justice amendment facts in SSC CGL Polity sections
